system_context.business_goals: To provide a global solution for the communication in the enterprise environment; increase the offering of services for enterprise communications.

architectures.name: Current Production Architecture (H.323-Based)
architectures.description: A client-server architecture built on the ITU-T H.323 protocol standard, designed to provide voice, data, and multimedia communication services over the company's Intranet. It uses dedicated server components (Gatekeeper, SiteKeeper) to manage call control, routing, and resource management for H.323 client terminals.
architectures.architecture_characteristics: Reliability, Standards-based Interoperability (H.323), Centralized Control, Potential Vendor/Protocol Lock-in.

key_components.responsibilities: Acts as a gateway for H.323 terminals (e.g., IP phones, video conferencing systems) to access the GON system. It multiplexes message requests and responses between terminals and the core network.
Implements core user-centric functionalities: User Authentication, Terminal Registration, Call Handling (setup, teardown), and triggering value-added services.
Serves as the primary interface between the Service Node (SN) and the access provider's network. It performs call routing and manages network resources (like bandwidth allocation).
The central call control component. It performs Address Translation (aliases to IP addresses), Admission Control (authorizing calls), and Bandwidth Control.

architectures.data_flow: 1. An H.323 terminal connects via the Terminal Agent (TA). 2. The User Agent (UA) handles user authentication and registration. 3. To make a call, the terminal's request is sent to the Gatekeeper (GK). 4. The GK performs admission control, translates the address, and interacts with the SiteKeeper (SK) for routing and resource allocation. 5. The SK establishes the path and manages the media stream between endpoints. All call control signaling flows through the central GK and SK.
architectural_constraints: Protocol Constraint: Core system communication is bound to the H.323 protocol suite.
Centralized Bottleneck: The Gatekeeper is a critical single point for call control; its failure affects system-wide call setup.
Terminal Homogeneity: Native support is optimized for H.323 terminals. Supporting other protocols (like SIP) requires additional gateway components not in the base architecture.
architectures.notes: This architecture is mature and stable for H.323 environments but is less flexible for integrating modern web-based protocols or decomposing into microservices. The proposed SIP architecture (A-02) represents a strategic shift to address these limitations.

scenario_elicitation.elicitation_method: AI-Driven Brainstorming based on System Context

scenarios.scenario_text: The system shall support at least 95% of successful call completions for a simultaneous test load of 500 H.323 client terminals (using Terminal Agent and User Agent software) placing point-to-point voice calls within the enterprise Intranet, as managed by the Gatekeeper and SiteKeeper components.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Directly matches. The current H.323 client-server architecture with Gatekeeper/SiteKeeper for call control and routing is explicitly designed for this. Feasibility is high, assuming proper capacity planning.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: This validates the core, non-negotiable requirement to provide reliable, global voice communication as a fundamental service.

scenarios.scenario_text: The system shall enable the addition of a new, integrated 'Persistent Group Chat' service within 6 months, allowing 50+ users to exchange text and files, with message history accessible for 90 days, without degrading existing voice/video call performance by more than 5%.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Indirect match/requires changes. The H.323 protocol and current components are optimized for real-time sessions (calls). Adding persistent, asynchronous messaging would likely require new components (e.g., a chat server, database) and significant extensions to the User Agent and potentially the Gatekeeper for user presence/management. Major architectural evolution.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Directly addresses the need to 'increase the offering of services' beyond basic voice/data/multimedia, expanding the communication portfolio to meet modern enterprise collaboration demands.

scenarios.scenario_text: The system shall support the integration of a third-party AI-based transcription service (via a defined API) to provide real-time captions for multimedia video conferences, with transcription latency under 2 seconds, without modifying the core H.323 protocol stack in the Terminal Agent.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Indirect match/requires changes. Feasible but requires architectural extension. The User Agent or a new 'Media Processing Agent' would need to handle audio stream forking/relay to the external service and reintegrate captions. The Gatekeeper might need new policies for routing/media handling. It's an additive feature that doesn't break the core architecture if designed as a plug-in.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Represents an exploratory enhancement to the 'multimedia services' offering, demonstrating system extensibility and the ability to incorporate innovative, value-added features that improve accessibility and utility.

scenarios.scenario_text: A System Administrator shall be able to deploy a security patch to the Gatekeeper component in a rolling fashion across a cluster of 3 servers, with zero downtime for active calls and less than 30 seconds of service interruption for new call routing requests.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Indirect match/requires changes. The current architecture description does not mention clustering or high-availability mechanisms. Achieving zero-downtime patching would require significant changes to the Gatekeeper's state management and failover design, likely introducing new components for load balancing and state replication.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Critical for maintaining the 'global solution' as a reliable enterprise service. Ensures system maintainability, availability, and operational stability without impacting the core communication service for end-users.

scenarios.scenario_text: A Maintainer shall be able to diagnose a call quality issue (e.g., high jitter) reported by a specific user by querying a centralized logging system to retrieve correlated logs from the user's Terminal Agent, the involved Gatekeeper, and the network path (via SiteKeeper) for the last 24 hours, within 5 minutes.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Indirect match/requires changes. The current architecture focuses on service delivery, not necessarily on integrated, cross-component observability. This scenario requires the addition of a centralized logging/monitoring component, standardized log formats across all agents and keepers, and potentially instrumentation changes in each component to expose the necessary metrics.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Essential for ensuring the quality and reliability of the 'global solution.' Reduces mean-time-to-resolution for issues, directly supporting service-level agreements and user satisfaction in a complex, distributed system.

scenario_classification.rationale: The scenario directly matches the core purpose and design of the current H.323-based architecture. The architecture is explicitly built to handle point-to-point voice calls between H.323 terminals via the Gatekeeper for call control and SiteKeeper for routing/resource management. The specified components (Terminal Agent, User Agent, Gatekeeper, SiteKeeper) are the primary actors in the documented data flow for call setup and management.

The current architecture is optimized for real-time, session-based communication (voice/video calls) using the H.323 protocol. Adding a persistent, asynchronous group chat service with message history is a fundamentally different communication paradigm. The existing components lack the necessary capabilities for persistent message storage, asynchronous delivery, group management, and long-term data retention.

While the core call handling might remain unchanged, integrating a real-time, external AI service for media processing (transcription) requires new capabilities not present in the base architecture. The architecture needs a mechanism to fork/relay audio media streams to an external service and reintegrate the results (captions) into the session, all while maintaining low latency and without modifying the core H.323 stack in the Terminal Agent.

The current architecture description presents the Gatekeeper as a central, singular call control component. The requirement for zero-downtime, rolling patching across a cluster implies high availability, stateful failover, and load balancing—capabilities not mentioned or inherent in the described centralized, monolithic design. The architecture lacks the redundancy and state management mechanisms needed to support this operational scenario.

The scenario requires integrated, cross-component observability and centralized log correlation for diagnostics. The current architecture is focused on service delivery, with no mention of a unified logging, monitoring, or telemetry system. Each component likely logs independently, and there is no defined mechanism to easily correlate logs across the Terminal Agent, Gatekeeper, and SiteKeeper for a specific user's call session.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: No architectural changes required. This is the primary use case the system was designed for. Success depends on proper capacity planning (server sizing, network bandwidth) and configuration, not on architectural modification.
issues_identified: Performance depends on capacity of central Gatekeeper and SiteKeeper. The 'Centralized Bottleneck' constraint is a relevant risk factor under high load.
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: N/A - The architecture natively supports this scenario.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: 1. Introduce new components: a 'Chat Server' to manage chat rooms, message routing, and persistence, and a 'Message Database' for 90-day history. 2. Extend the User Agent (UA) with a chat client interface and protocols (e.g., XMPP, WebSocket) to interact with the Chat Server. 3. Modify the Gatekeeper (GK) or integrate a new 'Presence Service' to manage chat user availability and roster information, potentially linking to existing UA authentication. 4. Ensure resource isolation so chat traffic does not degrade real-time call performance by more than 5%, possibly requiring QoS policies on SiteKeeper (SK).
issues_identified: Core H.323 protocol is not suited for chat, necessitating a parallel subsystem. Integration points (e.g., unified user identity between call and chat systems) are complex. The 6-month timeline is aggressive for a major architectural evolution. Risk of tightly coupling new services to the monolithic UA and GK, increasing their change sensitivity.
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: 1. Deploy the chat service as a completely separate, standalone application that shares only a common user directory (e.g., LDAP) with the H.323 system. This minimizes impact on the core architecture but fails the 'integrated' requirement. 2. Use a protocol gateway to bridge a standard chat protocol to a minimal extension within the UA, but this still requires significant new backend components.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: 1. Introduce a new 'Media Processing Agent' (MPA) component. Its role is to intercept/fork the audio RTP stream from a conference, send it to the third-party transcription API, receive text, and relay captions back to participants (e.g., via a separate data channel or embedded in video). 2. Modify the User Agent (UA) to initiate and control the transcription service for a session, likely via an API to the MPA. 3. Extend the Gatekeeper (GK) with policy rules to determine when transcription is required and to authorize/media-route calls involving the MPA. The Terminal Agent (TA) remains unchanged as per the requirement.
issues_identified: Introducing an external dependency (AI service) adds a new point of failure and latency. Ensuring sub-2-second latency requires low-latency media pathing and efficient API integration. The MPA becomes a critical resource for transcribed calls; its scalability must be considered. Modifying the GK for media policy adds complexity to this high-sensitivity component.
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Implement transcription as a client-side feature within a specific, advanced terminal software, completely bypassing the core network architecture. However, this would not be a 'system' integration, would lack central management, and may not work for all terminal types.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: 1. Redesign the Gatekeeper (GK) from a single/monolithic instance to a clustered, stateless (or shared-state) service. 2. Introduce a 'Load Balancer' (e.g., SIP-aware or H.323-aware) to distribute new call requests among GK instances. 3. Introduce a 'Shared State Database' (e.g., Redis, distributed cache) for call state, registration data, and admission control policies to enable failover. 4. Modify the SiteKeeper (SK) to interact with the GK cluster via virtual IP or service discovery. 5. Implement health checks and graceful draining mechanisms for rolling updates.
issues_identified: Transforming the 'Very High' sensitivity, central Gatekeeper into a distributed cluster is a profound architectural change. State management for active calls is particularly challenging to migrate without interruption. Risk of introducing new bottlenecks (shared state DB) and complexity. The 30-second interruption target for new calls might be difficult to achieve initially.
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: 1. Implement a cold-standby failover pair with manual or automated switchover for patching, accepting a brief full-service outage. This does not meet 'zero downtime for active calls'. 2. Schedule maintenance windows during low-usage periods, which conflicts with the 'global solution' goal of constant availability.

affected_components: New Component: Centralized Logging & Correlation System
scenario_evaluations.change_description: 1. Introduce a 'Centralized Logging System' (e.g., ELK stack, Splunk) as a new architectural component. 2. Standardize log formats across all components (TA, GK, SK) to include mandatory fields: session/call ID, user ID, terminal ID, timestamp, and relevant metrics (e.g., jitter, packet loss). 3. Instrument each component to export these structured logs and performance metrics to the central system. 4. Develop a correlation engine or dashboard that allows querying by user/session ID to retrieve all related logs across components within the 5-minute target.
issues_identified: Adding detailed logging/metrics collection may have a performance impact on the components, especially the high-sensitivity GK and SK. Retaining 24 hours of detailed, correlated logs for all calls requires significant storage. The 'network path' metrics via SiteKeeper may require deeper integration with network hardware or probes, which is outside the current component responsibilities.
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Rely on existing component-specific logs and manual correlation by an administrator using timestamps and IP addresses. This would not meet the 'within 5 minutes' requirement for complex diagnostics but would be a low-impact starting point.

scenario_interaction_weights.rationale: Gatekeeper is a central, high-sensitivity component with the highest scenario count (4). It is impacted by major changes: introducing chat (S-02) and transcription (S-03) adds integration complexity and external dependencies; clustering for high availability (S-04) requires a profound architectural redesign with high risk; and centralized logging (S-05) may impact performance. Failure to address these interactions risks system-wide outages, degraded user experience (e.g., call drops, latency), increased operational complexity, and inability to meet business requirements for scalability and resilience. The Gatekeeper's centrality makes it a single point of failure and a bottleneck for multiple critical evolutions.

User Agent is impacted by two scenarios: chat integration (S-02) and transcription services (S-03). Both require significant modifications to the UA (adding chat client interfaces, API integrations) and risk increasing its complexity and change sensitivity. If not addressed, end-users may lack required features (chat, real-time captions), impacting usability and compliance. Developers face increased maintenance burden due to tighter coupling with new subsystems. However, the UA's impact is more contained compared to the Gatekeeper, as changes are primarily client-side or involve new backend components rather than core infrastructure overhaul.

SiteKeeper is involved in high-availability clustering (S-04) and centralized logging (S-05). Changes include interacting with a GK cluster and instrumenting logs/metrics, which may affect performance and scalability. Not addressing these could hinder system resilience (e.g., failover support) and diagnostic capabilities, impacting operational efficiency and SLAs. However, SiteKeeper's role is more supportive—its changes are often driven by Gatekeeper evolution (e.g., clustering) or are additive (logging), so risks are somewhat secondary to those of the Gatekeeper. System administrators and maintainers are most impacted due to increased configuration and monitoring complexity.

architectural_strengths: Strength 1: The architecture excels at its core design purpose of real-time H.323-based video calling, as demonstrated by S-01 (Direct classification) requiring no architectural changes. This indicates a robust and focused foundation for its primary use case, with performance and reliability dependent primarily on operational capacity planning (server sizing, network bandwidth) rather than structural flaws.

Strength 2: The component-based structure (User Agent, Gatekeeper, SiteKeeper, Terminal Agent) provides clear separation of concerns, enabling targeted analysis of change impacts for new scenarios. This is evidenced by the ability to systematically identify affected components in each evaluation (e.g., S-02, S-03, S-04, S-05), facilitating a modular approach to evolution rather than requiring a complete system rewrite.
architectural_weaknesses: Weakness 1: The architecture exhibits high centralization and coupling around the monolithic Gatekeeper (GK), creating a single point of failure and a bottleneck for evolution. This is the primary 'Very High' sensitivity hotspot, directly impacting four scenarios (S-02, S-03, S-04, S-05). The need for a profound redesign into a clustered service for high availability (S-04, 'Widespread' change, 'High' risk) and its role as the integration nexus for new features like chat and transcription exposes significant rigidity and scalability limitations.

Weakness 2: The architecture is not inherently extensible for non-core communication paradigms, particularly data-centric services like chat (S-02). The H.323 protocol foundation is ill-suited for chat, forcing the introduction of a parallel subsystem ('Chat Server', 'Message Database') with complex integration points for user identity and presence. This results in 'Widespread' changes, 'High' effort/risk, and risks increasing the change sensitivity of core components like the UA and GK through tight coupling.

Weakness 3: The architecture lacks built-in cross-component observability and state management capabilities, requiring significant additive changes for operational needs. Implementing centralized logging/diagnostics (S-05) and shared state for clustering (S-04) necessitates introducing entirely new subsystems (Centralized Logging System, Shared State Database), indicating the original design did not prioritize modifiability for maintenance, resilience, or horizontal scaling.
overall_results.modifiability_assessment: The architecture demonstrates low to moderate modifiability; it is rigid and highly coupled around central components, making significant evolutionary changes (chat, clustering, new media services) high-effort, high-risk endeavors that risk destabilizing the core system, as evidenced by the 'Widespread' extent and 'High' risk levels for key scenarios.

recommendations.recommendation: Initiate a strategic refactoring of the Gatekeeper (GK) to decompose it into a set of coordinated, loosely coupled microservices (e.g., separate services for registration, call routing, policy management, presence) with a shared-nothing or shared-cache state model. This should be the first step before implementing S-04 (clustering) or deeply integrating new features (S-02, S-03).
recommendations.rationale: This directly addresses the central weakness and primary hotspot. Decoupling the GK's responsibilities reduces its change sensitivity, mitigates the single point of failure, and creates a more modular foundation. It enables independent scaling of functions (easing S-04), cleaner integration of new services (simplifying S-02 and S-03), and improves overall system resilience and modifiability.

recommendations.recommendation: Define and implement a clear, versioned internal API layer (e.g., gRPC, REST) for all inter-component communication, especially between the Gatekeeper, SiteKeeper, and new services. Mandate its use for all new integrations (e.g., Chat Server, Media Processing Agent).
recommendations.rationale: Introducing a formal API contract reduces tight coupling and hidden dependencies. It will contain the integration complexity for S-02 and S-03, making these subsystems more replaceable and their development more parallelizable. It also future-proofs the architecture by establishing a standard pattern for incorporating new capabilities, directly improving modifiability.

recommendations.recommendation: As a foundational quick win, implement the Centralized Logging & Correlation System (S-05) first, using a lightweight, non-intrusive agent-based approach. Use this new observability platform to instrument the GK refactoring and subsequent changes.
recommendations.rationale: This provides immediate operational benefit (meeting diagnostics requirements) and creates essential visibility into the system's behavior during the high-risk architectural transformations. It is a 'plumbing' improvement that enhances modifiability by making the impact of future changes more measurable and debuggable, supporting the higher-priority GK decomposition.

recommendations.recommendation: For the chat integration (S-02), adopt the alternative approach of a standalone chat service that shares only a common user directory (e.g., LDAP), with a lightweight gateway/bridge in the UA for integrated UI. Defer deep protocol-level integration.
recommendations.rationale: This minimizes immediate risk and architectural disruption to the core H.323 system, aligning with the aggressive 6-month timeline. It accepts a temporary compromise on 'integration' depth to avoid high-risk coupling, allowing the core GK refactoring to proceed. This improves modifiability by keeping the chat system as a separate, replaceable module that can be more tightly integrated later once the core is more stable.
